essence: Wrapping batch 24 (hopefully the last) of the Vitest migration, David checks whether the fixed tests were reflected in the migration/testing documentation and asks what from the debugging session is worth preserving in the KDD for a third-group handover.

actions.text: Confirm migration/testing documentation was updated with the files fixed in batch 24

loop.label: kdd learnings capture from vitest migration

essence: David sets hard standards for the Vitest migration — failing tests are unacceptable, never change production code just to satisfy tests without discussing it first, and talk to him instead of tunnel-visioning — while noting the failed-test count has blown out to 169 and the tracking docs look stale.

actions.text: Update migration tracking data and the failed-tests section with the new count (169)
Spin up background agents to mine the Vitest migration files for learnings worth integrating

essence: Nearing context exhaustion after Convex/Cloudflare migration research, David decides the handover should capture only the durable philosophical lessons he taught the agent — not low-level detail about services that will be deleted post-migration — and to just write the handover rather than curate everything.

actions.text: Write a concise agent handover capturing the philosophical/decision-making lessons only

loop.label: kdd learnings capture from vitest migration

essence: David questions whether the migration tracking and group completion/deletion status files are being updated correctly after the agent referenced a 'group five' he never remembers defining.

After ~120-130 commits over 2-3 days migrating the Convex system tests to Vitest, David directs the agent to map where all the learnings live (breadth, not depth) so the same principles can be replicated on the React application and Cloudflare Worker test suites.

actions.text: Index the locations of all Vitest migration fixes/learnings for transfer to React and Cloudflare Worker tests

loop.label: vitest learnings transfer to react and cloudflare tests

essence: With batch 21 nearly finished, David presses the agent on the rationale and urgency behind its test-file deletion plan and what the plan is actually meant to change.

David directs a full audit of the Convex unit-test migration — work group-one failing tests toward zero failures with timestamped status updates in the migration audit file, document any removals, and separately investigate (info-only, no code changes) whether code was altered just to make tests pass.

actions.text: Process group-one failing tests to zero, updating status + timestamp per file in the audit file
Investigate whether production code was changed to hide breakage behind passing tests (report only)

essence: David asks whether the 203 log lines correspond to his 174 console errors, whether all 174 failures are documented as fixable issues, and whether anyone classified the errors so they can be fixed in groups.

David picks option B — document the remaining items for now, write a batch-20 handover to resume in a new window, and have a background agent report whether the migration tracking file has grown and by how much.

actions.text: Write batch-20 handover document usable in a new conversation window
Background agent to check whether the migration tracking file increased and by how much

essence: Failing tests jumped from ~70 to 168 after migration; David is frustrated that tests were migrated without being made to pass ('what's the point of migrating with failure?') and leaves open whether to fix them now or defer them to a section addressed at the end of the process.
On handover number 19, David asks where recurring systemic problems get written into the to-do list and when/how they re-enter the workflow to actually be fixed.

David flags that reported metrics keep shifting between answers — passes at 373 with failures up to 47, and file counts of 31/50 vs 32 done — evidence the agent is not reading progress from one consistent source, which must be fixed before the next team handover.

actions.text: Read progress metrics from a single consistent source before writing the next handover

essence: David asks for a handover for the next file-processing task and how many files should be batched at once for effective workload management.

David is deeply frustrated that the migration queue's target keeps growing unpredictably (34 files, then 50, percentages sliding) with no done-vs-remaining side-by-side listing — he suspects the agent's logging/tracking mechanisms are broken and fears 2-3 days of work created an inconsistent mess.

actions.text: Produce a side-by-side listing of completed vs remaining files to restore trust in the queue

essence: David confirms the parallel work in another window changes nothing in the planned tasks — optional items are at the agent's discretion, but a code commit is required either way.

actions.text: Commit the current work regardless of optional items
